coredata.dc:title: Substrate interaction of graphene quantum dots in solution studied by hyperpolarized NMR
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Zefan
Hilty, Christian
coredata.dc:description: The reversible adsorption of CO2-derived species to graphene quantum dots in CO2 electroreduction reaction was characterized by nuclear spin hyperpolarized NMR. Hyperpolarization of 13C-labeled sodium bicarbonate by the dissolution dynamic nuclear polarization (DNP) technique provided a 5000-fold signal enhancement and enabled real-time and in-situ 13C NMR experiments. The R 1 and R 2 relaxation rates were measured using time-resolved DNP-NMR experiments, and the bound species were confirmed to be carbonic acid and its conjugates. A significant difference in the relaxation rates of bound and free species was found, indicating a reversible binding. In addition to relaxation rates, kinetic rate constants were fitted from the small-flip angle experiments and compared to theoretical calculations. The pH-dependent adsorption and desorption rate constants were found in the range of 3.5–9.5 and 0.7–2.0 s−1, with the average binding distance at 279 - 291 pm. The DNP-NMR technique was demonstrated as an effective way to study surface adsorption, with potential in electrochemistry and kinetics applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Nuclear magnetic resonance
Hyperpolarization

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis and characterization of GQD samples

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Financial Support from the Welch Foundation (Grant A-1658 ) is gratefully acknowledged. Assistance with TEM and AFM from Dr. Chun Hsin Kuo and Dr. Wilson Serem of the Texas A&M Materials Characterization Facility is greatly appreciated.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Normalized 13C NMR spectra of DNP polarized bicarbonate mixed with buffer solution of pH 2.63, 4.30, 6.10, 8.95, 11.45, from bottom to top, respectively, a) in the presence and b) absence of preloaded GQD. The spectra are normalized and referenced to the ethylene glycol peak intensity at 63.7 ppm, which is part of the hyperpolarized solvent. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

a)Time-resolved slices of 13C NMR spectra of CPMG experiments at pH = 4.30. b) The fitting of the normalized time-dependent peak integral of the bound peak at 159.5 ppm (Yellow; bottom) and free CO2(aq) peak at 124.7 ppm (Blue; top) and the free bicarbonate peak at 160.5 ppm (Green; middle). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Fitted curve overlaid on the integral of free CO2(aq) peak (Blue), bound peak(Yellow), and free peak (Green) in small-flip angle experiment at pH of a) 4.30, b) 6.10, c) 8.95 and d) 11.45. The corresponding fitted curves (light color) were generated using the start of mixing as the zero time mark. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Calculated relaxation rates attributed to different mechanisms compared to experimental values for a) Bound peak R 1, b) Free peak R 1, c) Bound peak R 2, d) Free peak R 2. The error of experimental values is given by the standard deviation of three parallel experiments. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

The fitted R 2 relaxation rates from CPMG experiments. The bound peak at 159.5 ppm and the free bicarbonate/carbonate peak at 160.5 ppm were measured. The error was obtained from the standard deviation of three parallel experiments.

The fitted R 1 relaxation rates from small-flip angle experiments. The bound peak at 159.5 ppm and the free bicarbonate/carbonate peak at 160.5 ppm were measured. The error was obtained from the standard deviation of three separate experiments.
The fitted dipolar distance and radius from theoretical calculations.

head.ce:title: Substrate interaction of graphene quantum dots in solution studied by hyperpolarized NMR

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Chemistry Department, Texas A&M University, College Station, TX, 77843, USA
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Chemistry Department, Texas A&M University, College Station, TX, 77843, USA

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The reversible adsorption of CO2-derived species to graphene quantum dots in CO2 electroreduction reaction was characterized by nuclear spin hyperpolarized NMR. Hyperpolarization of 13C-labeled sodium bicarbonate by the dissolution dynamic nuclear polarization (DNP) technique provided a 5000-fold signal enhancement and enabled real-time and in-situ 13C NMR experiments. The R 1 and R 2 relaxation rates were measured using time-resolved DNP-NMR experiments, and the bound species were confirmed to be carbonic acid and its conjugates. A significant difference in the relaxation rates of bound and free species was found, indicating a reversible binding. In addition to relaxation rates, kinetic rate constants were fitted from the small-flip angle experiments and compared to theoretical calculations. The pH-dependent adsorption and desorption rate constants were found in the range of 3.5–9.5 and 0.7–2.0 s−1, with the average binding distance at 279 - 291 pm. The DNP-NMR technique was demonstrated as an effective way to study surface adsorption, with potential in electrochemistry and kinetics applications.

ce:section.ce:para: CO2 electroreduction reactions have shown promise for providing the chemical industry with cheap and clean raw organic precursors and fuel, and for fixation of atmospheric CO2 [1,2]. Graphene has been demonstrated as a versatile substrate material to produce catalysts for CO2 electroreduction reactions [3–6]. Graphene-bound metal atoms can form active sites similar to those found in organometallic complexes. Graphene materials also offer the potential for designing new types of active sites. Due to the heterogeneous nature of these materials, it is still unclear whether the catalytic activity is attributed to metal-free sites or to trace amounts of bound metal [7].

Graphene quantum dots (GQD) are reported to form the basis of an electrocatalyst family for CO2 electroreduction [8–10]. Among them, nitrogen-doped graphene (N-GQD) stands out as an option [11]. Bottom-up pyrolysis synthesis is a viable route for the synthesis of graphene of small particle size, producing optimizable graphene quantum dots [12,13]. The mechanism of CO2 reduction reactions on these catalysts is still in debate [9]. For graphene synthesized by a modified Hummers' method, it is presumed in past studies that the CO2 electroreduction reaction in aqueous solution happens primarily as bicarbonate or carbonate anions form [14–16]. To determine the identity of the intermediates, computational models have been adopted to calculate the most stable structures of the intermediates at the rate-determining steps using density-functional theory (DFT) [17,18]. In contrast to the aforementioned reports, the first step of the CO2 electroreduction reaction is reported to be CO2 molecule adsorption, with bond formation between a dopant active-site atom and the carbon atom of CO2. Yet, ambiguity regarding the nature of the binding species exists, with many studies not distinguishing between bicarbonate and gaseous CO2 substrate [19]. Direct real-time and in-situ experimental evidence for CO2 electroreduction mechanisms is still needed. Finding the mechanism and the intermediate structures of these catalyzed reactions, as well as the kinetic rate constants, are imperative for the more efficient development of electrocatalysts.

13C NMR has seen extensive use in various branches of chemistry, including the identification and quantification of chemical species in catalysis [20]. Nuclear spin hyperpolarization has been proposed to enhance the NMR signal to solve the sensitivity issue that can arise in the detection of low-γ nuclei such as 13C. Dynamic nuclear polarization (DNP) stands out as a powerful technique that is capable of hyperpolarizing nuclear spins in a wide range of compounds [21]. Dissolution dynamic nuclear polarization (D-DNP) generates a non-equilibrium, hyperpolarized nuclear spin state by utilizing the polarization of electron spins on paramagnetic radical species at low temperature and microwave irradiation to transfer nuclear spins through electron-nucleus hyperfine coupling [22]. As such, D-DNP produces a large signal enhancement for NMR spectroscopy, which enables 13C NMR detection at low concentrations and on fast timescales. The D-DNP NMR technique has demonstrated the capability for the in-situ and real-time characterization of molecular interactions, molecular dynamics, and the measurement of reaction kinetics and mechanisms [23,24].

In this work, we characterize the adsorption/desorption interaction of bicarbonate-derived species with GQD. We use hyperpolarized NMR for the real-time measurement of the relaxation processes concomitant with kinetic rates. Finally, we compare the results to NMR relaxation calculations for determining dynamics and distances characterizing the interaction.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of GQD samples
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis of GQD loosely followed the protocol described in the reported pyrolysis synthesis [12]. Two grams of anhydrous citric acid (Amresco, Solon, OH) was weighed in a Teflon container and transferred into a preheated oven (FED 115-UL; Binder, Tuttlingen, Germany) and kept at 200 °C for 45 min. The viscous molten orange liquid was then immediately poured into 100 ml of a cold aqueous solution of 1 M sodium hydroxide (Sigma-Aldrich, St. Louis, MO) and stirred overnight. The pH of the solution was neutralized by titration with 1 M hydrochloric acid (VWR International, Radnor, PA). Dialysis cartridges (Pierce Slide-A-Lyzer, 10 kDa MWCO, 150-250 ml; Thermo Scientific, Waltham, MA) were used to deionize the resulting brownish solution. A stable orange GQD solution was thus obtained. Additional samples of nitrogen-doped GQD (N-GQD) were synthesized with the following alternative method. Two milliliters of 4 M citric acid solution was mixed with 540 μL ethylenediamine (VWR International), stirred, and let cool for 10 min. The resulting colorless liquid was irradiated in a microwave oven with 350 W power for 3 min. The resulting viscous molten orange liquid was immediately poured into 10 ml water and stirred overnight. The solution was neutralized with the same 1 M sodium hydroxide solution and dialyzed using the same procedures described above. A stable N-GQD solution was obtained. The GQD and N-GQD samples were used at 20 g/L concentration.

The GQD samples were characterized by UV–visible spectroscopy (UV–vis), fluorescence spectroscopy, transmission electron microscopy (TEM), atomic force microscopy (AFM), and electron paramagnetic resonance spectroscopy (EPR). The UV–vis spectra were measured in a BioSpec-mini spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Columbia, MA). The fluorescence spectra were acquired with a QuantaMaster40 spectrophotometer (Photon Technology International, Edison, NJ). The TEM images were measured with a Titan Themis 300 S Platform (FEI Company, Hillsboro, OR), and the AFM images with an Anasys Instruments nanoIR2-s (Bruker, Billerica, MA). EPR spectra were acquired with an ELEXSYS-II E500 CW-EPR machine (Bruker, Billerica, MA).

Samples for DNP hyperpolarization consisted of 300 mM 13C labeled NaHCO3 (Cambridge Isotope Laboratories Inc., Andover, MA), 15 mM OX63 radical (Oxford Instruments, Abingdon, UK), and 0.5 mM Gd-DTPA (GE Healthcare, Chicago, IL) in a 3:2 (v/v) mixture of ethylene glycol (VWR International) and water, to form a glassy mixture upon freezing. Aliquots of 40 μl volume were hyperpolarized in a HyperSense DNP polarizer (Oxford Instruments) at a temperature of 1.4 K, with a 60 mW microwave irradiation at a frequency of 93.974 GHz. After 105 min of hyperpolarization, the samples were dissolved by a dissolution injection with 4 ml water, preheated to a pressure of 0.6 MPa and 125 °C. A dissolved sample was transferred to a storage loop of a homemade automatic gas injector device [25]. The first 400 μL of the sample was injected into a 400 MHz NMR spectrometer (Bruker Biospin, Billerica, MA) equipped with a 5 mm Broadband Observe (BBO) probe. The injection was driven by nitrogen gas at a forward pressure of 1.81 MPa against a back pressure of 1.01 MPa. In the NMR tube, the hyperpolarized solution mixed with 50 μl preloaded sample. For different experiments, the preloaded sample contained 300 mM buffer solution in water, with or without 10 mg/ml GQD or N-GQD solution. NMR measurements were then triggered after a stabilization time of 500 ms. The temperature of the mixed sample was measured to be 298 K. The buffer solution was prepared by mixing sodium hydroxide (Sigma-Aldrich, St. Louis, MO) in a 1:2 M ratio with acids, including phosphoric acid (pH = 2.60), acetic acid (pH = 4.30), monosodium phosphate (pH = 6.10), ammonium chloride (pH = 8.95) and disodium phosphate (pH = 11.45) (All from Sigma-Aldrich, St. Louis, MO), and diluted to 600 mM.

13C NMR spectroscopy experiments were performed immediately after the mixing time of 0.5 s. Single scan pulse-acquire experiments started with a π/2 hard pulse of 9.95 μs duration applied at a center frequency of 125 ppm, and 13C spectra were acquired. In small-flip angle experiments, a hard pulse with π/18 flip angle was followed by data acquisition and a spoiling pulsed field gradient. The Carr-Purcell-Meiboom-Gill (CPMG) pulse sequence started with three selective excitation pulses and pulsed field gradients on three axes to suppress the ethylene glycol signal, followed by a p1-[τ-2p1-τ]× n sequence, with p1 being hard pulse with x phase, and τ a delay of 13.235 ms. The receiver was unblanked and signal acquisition was performed during each interval of τ. This pulse sequence allowed the acquisition of an entire echo train and determination of the R 2 relaxation rate with a single scan.

The DNP-NMR data were processed using Python (Python Software Foundation, www.python.org). Zero filling was performed by adding points to the start and the end of echoes in CPMG experiments or after the signal in other experiments, doubling the data length. An exponential window function of 6 Hz line-broadening was applied to the pulse-acquire and small-flip angle data, and a sine wave window function was applied to CPMG data. Phase correction was performed to minimize the imaginary component of the complex CPMG data or to obtain pure absorption spectra in the other experiments. The time-resolved integrals of peaks were then collected from integrating peak regions across all slices and scans of the datasets.

The processed DNP-NMR data were further analyzed in Python. The small-flip angle experiments employed Lorentzian fitting to obtain integrals from closely spaced peaks. A model of simultaneous kinetic conversion and spin relaxation based on chemical population and Solomon differential equations was adopted to simulate the real-time evolution of the NMR signal (Fig. S1). The kinetic and relaxation rate constants were fitted to the least squared difference of the numerical solutions of the model equation and the time-dependent DNP-NMR peak integrals. The CPMG results were fitted to an exponential function,
I =
I 0
e
−
R 2
t
. Furthermore, spin relaxation rates of the bound and free CO2 species were calculated based on contributions from dipolar relaxation and chemical exchange (Equations S8-13). The theoretical relaxation rates were calculated individually for the bicarbonate-derived species, and were subsequently summed with weighting by their population proportions at all experimental pH levels (Table S5). The interatomic distances and dynamic parameters were fitted to relaxation rates of bound and free bicarbonate-derived species from the theoretical equations obtained above.

The GQD used in the experiments were characterized with spectroscopy and microscopy methods (Figs. S2–3) to demonstrate their nanoparticle nature and their consistency with other published work [12,26,27]. The narrow 365 nm absorption peaks confirmed the existence of heteroatom-sp2 bonds from the pyrolysis-leftover hydroxyl and carboxyl groups on GQD. The consistent 454 nm fluorescence emission wavelength confirmed the presence of uniformly sized sp2 clusters. A TEM image of higher resolution of this aggregated sample corroborated the nanometer size of GQD samples, giving an average size of 3.01 nm and a standard deviation of 0.48 nm. Clusters of GQD particles were observed on AFM images, confirming the thickness of GQD few layer structure to be 0.5 nm. Due to the fluorescent nature originating from the few-nanometer quantum confinement effect [12], this aggregation was considered to happen during microscopy sample preparation and should not be considered to take place during liquid-state NMR experiments. EPR spectroscopy confirmed no presence of paramagnetic radical or transition metal ions in the GQD, leaving oxygen and nitrogen-containing moieties as the only candidates for active sites.

The interaction of GQD with bicarbonate-derived species was measured starting from hyperpolarized bicarbonate substrate. The carbonic acid conjugates were formed by the mixing of hyperpolarized bicarbonate solution with the sample containing a buffer at the respective pH between 2.60 and 11.45 (Fig. 1 a). In the 13C NMR spectra, the peak at 125 ppm was attributed to CO2(aq), and the peak at 160.5–168 ppm to bicarbonate/carbonate, consistent with reported chemical shift values [28]. The bicarbonate and carbonate in aqueous solution were reported to undergo a fast exchange, and only one peak with a pH-dependent chemical shift could be observed in the 13C NMR spectrum [28]. The bicarbonate and carbonate are therefore treated as a single species during the NMR relaxation studies. The hyperpolarized NMR experiments yielded a much stronger signal compared to conventional NMR experiments and enabled an enhancement in signal-to-noise ratio of 5000 in this experiment, ensuring a high observable signal in ensuing NMR experiments.

The peak intensity of CO2(aq) and carbonic acid conjugates in the GQD-free control experiments in Fig. 1 showed peak integrals and species population distribution at measured pH levels. Stronger bicarbonate/carbonate peaks due to the absence of adsorption-caused consumption at a pH of 6.10–11.45 were observed. This is consistent with the reported populations and corroborates the peak assignment above [28]. A weaker free peak was observed at pH 4.30 and 2.60, however, which can be attributed to a lower conversion rate [29,30]. Furthermore, the chemical shift of the bicarbonate/carbonate peak was found to agree with the bicarbonate/carbonate distribution following a titration curve consistent with the reported pKa values [30].

In the presence of preloaded GQD, an additional signal at 159.5 ppm was observed (Fig. 1b). This signal, which showed no pH-dependence in chemical shift, was hypothesized to stem from the bicarbonate or a derived carbonic acid conjugate species because of their rapid and independent appearance after mixing despite the relatively slow CO2 generation reaction. This peak had a strong NMR signal intensity, indicating a fast kinetic process that occurred during the 500 ms mixing time prior to signal acquisition. The rate constant of this conversion was estimated to be on the same magnitude as the bicarbonate to CO2(aq) reaction, as these two products were found to evolve at the same time. The chemical shift of the newly appeared peak was found to be independent of pH, suggesting that the carbon atom may bind directly to a moiety that provides a pH-independent shielding effect.

Further information regarding the identity of the additional signal in the presence of GQD was found by quantitative fitting for spin-spin (R 2) and spin-lattice relaxation (R 1) rates and kinetic rate constants of all involved chemical species.

A series of time-resolved DNP-NMR CPMG spectra slices, along with integrals of the bicarbonate and the additional signal at pH 4.30, are shown in Fig. 2 . The GQD-associated peak and a peak of free CO2(aq) were observed at 159.5 and 124.7 ppm, respectively, at this acidic condition, as well as a small bicarbonate peak at 160.5 ppm. This GQD-associated peak demonstrated a much faster relaxation and was interpreted to be a bound species. A fast relaxation is a clear indication of a prolonged correlation time due to the binding of small CO2 molecules and/or carbonic acid conjugate anions to a much larger GQD particle. In contrast to the bound species, the free bicarbonate/carbonate peak was considered to constitute the free peak.

The pH dependency and proton availability had been perceived as an important factor for such binding and subsequent CO2 electroreduction, which had been reported to demonstrate a great pH-dependent selectivity [31,32]. Therefore, experiments at pH levels of 4.30, 6.10, 8.95, and 11.45 were conducted to simulate the possible future electrochemistry application scenarios.

The R 2 relaxation rates were fitted from CPMG experiment results and are shown in Table 1 . In addition, the R 2 relaxation rate of CO2(aq) was fitted to be 0.138 ± 0.013 s−1 at pH = 4.30. A significant contrast between the R 2 of bound peaks and free peaks was observed. At pH 4.30, an 8-fold difference in R 2 was observed, suggesting a binding interaction that increases the correlation time and the relaxation rates due to the dipolar relaxation mechanism. At higher pH, the distinction was observed to be less apparent. This was attributed to a lower relative contribution from the dipolar mechanism larger contribution from the scalar mechanism, which is less affected by a change in correlation time.

Quantitative results for R 1 relaxation rates and kinetic rate constants were fitted from the time-resolved integrals of small-flip angle experiments, as demonstrated in Fig. 3 . The observation of in-situ and real-time NMR spectra was achieved by applying the small-flip angle pulse while leaving much of the polarization intact. As a result, the bound peak, free bicarbonate/carbonate peak, and free CO2(aq) peak were monitored in the course of the ongoing reaction. The as-acquired NMR signal is a function of time, from which both the R 1 relaxation rates and kinetic rate constants were fitted. In the figure, the fitted curves were generated from the start of the mixing, the de facto start point of the chemical reaction when bicarbonate and GQD in buffer solution met.

A kinetic model comprising three species, free carbonate, bound carbonate, and CO2, was fitted to the small-flip angle data (Fig. S1). The signals of these species are subject to change due to R 1 relaxation causing a net loss of magnetization per species, and kinetic rates causing an inter-conversion (Equation S6). In the time-resolved integrals, the maximum intensity of the bound peak appeared before the free CO2(aq) peak. The slow kinetic process of CO2-carbonic acid conversion suggests that the bound species is carbonic acid and its conjugates, not CO2(aq). Consequently, in the model, rate constants for the conversion between free CO2(aq) and bound bicarbonate/carbonate were set to zero. The initial concentrations reflected the production of hyperpolarized bicarbonate before the start of the experiment. The as-fitted results are listed in Table 2 (individual fits in Table S1).

The fitted R 1 relaxation rates are close to the R 2 rates, as expected for small molecules, with a significant difference between bound and free peaks observed. A similar trend in pH dependency was also observed, which can be attributed to the dipolar relaxation contribution described above.

The pseudo first-order adsorption rate constants of 3.5–9.5 and 0.7–2.0 s−1 correspond to a second-order rate constant of 0.35–0.95 L g−1 s−1 in the low free species limit. These rate constants are primarily determined by the magnitude of the bound signal at the beginning of the data acquisition after the mixing time. A favoring of the bound state is consistent with the observation of strong bound peak NMR signals. The kinetic rates were higher at higher pH levels, which is arguably caused by deprotonation of surface active sites on GQD. In addition, the bicarbonate to CO2 rate constant was fitted to be 1.56 ± 0.36 s−1, and CO2 to bicarbonate 0.55 ± 0.11 s−1. In comparison, these rate constants were previously reported to be 0.70 and 0.18 s−1, respectively, at 293K for this pH level [33]. However, many weak acid anions may serve as catalysts for this reversible reaction [34], and the increased rates could be attributed to the surface moieties on the GQD.

A set of control experiments was performed to investigate the potential contributions of paramagnetic species involved in the hyperpolarization process to the overall spin relaxation rates (Tables S7–9). It was found that the paramagnetic Gd-DTPA had no significant impact on the general relaxation rates and that the GQD had no significant effect on the relaxation rates of free CO2(aq) and free bicarbonate/carbonate species.

The bond length and GQD particle size were fitted from modeling the nuclear spin relaxation of bound and free species. It was assumed that the 13C nuclear relaxation was exclusively caused by 1H-13C dipolar and chemical kinetics-induced scalar mechanisms. The free and bound species were enclosed in a rigid hydration sphere, with both intramolecular hydrogen and hydration-water hydrogen contributing to the dipolar mechanism (Equations S8-13).

The free bicarbonate had a fitted 1H-13C dipolar distance of 195 pm, very close to the reported theoretical value of 194 pm [36]. The hydration water hydrogens of free species were fitted to be 220 pm away from the carbon, similar to the reported theoretical value of 2 Å [37]. The agreement in the data of the free species can serve to validate the model.

The radius of the bound species with the GQD particle was fitted to be 0.81–0.92 nm, which is smaller than the average values of 3.01 nm from TEM images. This difference can readily be explained by non-rigid binding of the substrates to the particle surface, which results in a shorter correlation time. It is further possible that binding to smaller particles is more favorable.

The bound species at basic pH, i.e., bound carbonate, resulted in an average intramolecular 1H-13C distance of around 290 pm, which was presumed to be the distance of the central carbon atom to the closest hydrogen atom on the GQD. The fitted intramolecular 1H-13C distance was much shorter for H2CO3, close to that of free species, suggesting that protonated carboxyl groups exist in close proximity to bound sites at the more acidic conditions. Overall, a close carbon-proton distance was observed in the fitted data, suggesting that the carbonate binds to proton-containing moieties on the GQD surface.

The hydration-water 1H-13C distance of bound species was fitted to be larger than for the free species. This difference may be due to a change in the hydrogen bonding pattern, as well as the number of protons in the hydration shell, here estimated as half of a complete shell, and a faster motion of hydration protons, which could also result in a larger fitted distance.

The relative binding affinity was observed to be pH independent, with k(free→bound)/k(bound→free) = 4.85 ± 0.40, 4.55 ± 0.52, 4.01 ± 0.61 and 4.71 ± 0.57, for pH levels of 4.30, 6.10, 8.95 and 11.45 respectively, corresponding to an average association constant for the second order reaction of 0.45 L g−1. However, the adsorption/desorption rate was found to increase at increasing pH levels. This suggests that using acidic or basic conditions for CO2 electroreduction reactions would be possible, and an ideal pH level should be determined by subsequent electrochemistry mechanisms.

The effectiveness of N-GQD was also investigated (Table S10). The difference in binding properties of carbonic acid conjugates to N-GQD was found to be insignificant. As a result, the distances and kinetics are predicted to be similar to that of regular GQDs. The reported increased N-GQD catalytic activity may be attributed to additional transition metal ions present on the doped particles [7].

With hyperpolarized NMR, carbonic acid and its conjugates were found to bind reversibly to GQD that was synthesized by the pyrolysis method. The average distance to a nearest proton in the binding site and kinetic rates was found to have pH dependency, with a shorter distance in acidic solution and a faster reversible binding in basic solution. However, the association constant remained largely pH independent, implying that the potential use of the particles for electroreduction reactions could extend over a broad pH range. Furthermore, the application of hyperpolarized liquid state NMR in characterizing electrochemical reaction mechanisms was demonstrated.

Zefan Zhang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Christian Hilty: Conceptualization, Methodology, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Financial Support from the Welch Foundation (Grant A-1658) is gratefully acknowledged. Assistance with TEM and AFM from Dr. Chun Hsin Kuo and Dr. Wilson Serem of the Texas A&M Materials Characterization Facility is greatly appreciated.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Advances and challenges for the electrochemical reduction of CO2 to CO: from fundamentals to industrialization

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Jin, S.; Hao, Z.; Zhang, K.; Yan, Z.; Chen, J. Advances and Challenges for the Electrochemical Reduction of CO2 to CO: From Fundamentals to Industrialization. Angewandte Chemie 2021, 133 (38), 20795-20816. https://doi.org/10.1002/ange.202101818.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: What would it take for renewably powered electrosynthesis to displace petrochemical processes?

Navalon, S.; Dhakshinamoorthy, A.; Alvaro, M.; Garcia, H. Carbocatalysis by Graphene-Based Materials. Chem. Rev. 2014, 114 (12), 6179-6212. https://doi.org/10.1021/cr4007347.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Synthesis of graphene-supported noble metal hybrid nanostructures and their applications as advanced electrocatalysts for fuel cells

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Identifying active sites of nitrogen-doped carbon materials for the CO2 reduction reaction

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Incorporation of nitrogen defects for efficient reduction of CO2 via two-electron pathway on three-dimensional graphene foam

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Wu, J.; Liu, M.; Sharma, P. P.; Yadav, R. M.; Ma, L.; Yang, Y.; Zou, X.; Zhou, X.-D.; Vajtai, R.; Yakobson, B. I.; Lou, J.; Ajayan, P. M. sb:title.sb:maintitle: Manganese deception on graphene and implications in catalysis

sb:title.sb:maintitle: How copper catalyzes the electroreduction of carbon dioxide into hydrocarbon fuels
